comment_content:
This is probably going to be unpopular but I think fictionalising things to the point where he seems like an innocent victim who wanted to do good for the world in the end (by helping to catch ted bundy, wich didn't happen at all in real life) is doing the world a disservice. 
I know that it's a tv show but I just think that when you use real life people, you have a responsability to the victims. And I think it was incredibly disrespectful to the victims by making bernice seem like a sex obsessed crazy person who didn't want to take no for an awnser and even calling her the town bicycle, when in real life there is absolutely no indication of this at all, not even that they went on a date. And they used her actual name. Same goes with the real life girl he allegedly dated, they make her out to be not only an accomplice but even an instigator on a some of the gruesome acts Gein actualy did, when again there is absolutely no indication of this in real life. They make him more sympathetic whilst making the victims seem a little less innocent and this causes a lot of people to disconnect from reality and sometimes even idolize an actual killer. Same happened with dahmer, a lot of people saying that they thought he was cute and felt bad for him, when he killed, dismembered and ate over 17 people (some of them children).
reply_content: It's not about aiming to get him fervor or special consideration for the atrocities he committed. In no way, shape, or form does this show paint him as a victim, despite the very real fact that he was a victim of schizophrenic bipolar and antisocial personality. It's providing relevant reasons as to why he ended up the way he did. Do not mistake making excuses, which this show isn't, for merely providing possible reasons for what lead to him doing what he did. It doesn't glorify it in any way.

comment_content: I'm quite familiar with Ed Gein.  I have Harold Schechter's book on Ed Gein.  Artistic license aside, Ed didn't sound like the way the actor portrayed -- just kind of FYI for those interested, but I think the odd voice is a method of conveying him as an "oddball", not an attempt to mirror his actual voice.  You can hear Ed Gein's voice on interview tapes via the docuseries, "Psycho:  The Lost Tapes of Ed Gein".  Much of the storyline in the Netflix series is made up.  For example, there was no girl that he was dating that found Bernice Worden's body.  Police found her when they entered the shed.  Not a bad Netflix show, but largely "artistic", with some facts sprinkled throughout the show.  If you really want the raw facts, read Harold Schechter's book, "Deviant".  If you found that interesting, get his book called "Fiend" - it's another true crime novel he wrote, about Jesse Pomeroy.  Though I have yet to read it, "Bestial" is one of his acclaimed books as well.
reply_content: Yeah, this show is okay (I mean it’s typical Murphy) but it’s wildly exaggerating events and depicting things—even murders—that did not happen. Entertaining and disturbing but this show is not meant for anyone who wants the true story.
